# Tiger L1 — Mei (OSS Maintainer) × scan-assess

**Verdict: fix-first.** The no-signup public path and the honest mock labelling are real, but on the most likely free-tier deployment (`LLM_PROVIDER=auto`, no key) the "AI-scored" badge is generated by a deterministic `if/else` catalog, not a model — and the report disclosure of that fact is a corner chip, not the prominent caveat it is everywhere else. As Mei, a badge whose roadmap was templated and whose "AI auditor" never ran is a vibe with a logo. I'd not paste it until the public tier provably runs a real model (the cheap `gemini-3-flash` floor already wired in clears my bar) or the mock state is disclosed as loudly as a degrade.

## Angle & reachable output (tier-honest)

Mei is the **free, anonymous, no-signup public scan** Character. What she actually reaches: `/` → `/report/[owner]/[repo]` → `/badge`, all account-free (confirmed: the login wall at `src/app/api/scan/stream/route.ts:56` only fires for `orgSlug !== "public"`; `authGateEnabled` gates private/org only, `src/lib/access.ts:44`). So her no-signup criterion **passes** — a genuine strength.

The question my angle forces: **on the free path, which engine actually produces her badge — a real model or the mock floor?** Traced:

- Public scan sets `useLLM: !mock = true` (`stream/route.ts:132`) — the scan *wants* an LLM.
- The engine is whatever `getProvider()` returns from `LLM_PROVIDER`. Default is `auto` (`src/lib/llm/index.ts:62`). `auto` → `geminiOrMock()` → **`key ? GeminiProvider : MockProvider`** (`index.ts:70-73, 140-143`). A free public deploy with **no `GEMINI_API_KEY`/`GOOGLE_API_KEY` runs the deterministic MockProvider** for every public scan.
- And it runs it as a **first-class success, not a degrade.** Because `getProvider` returns MockProvider directly, `intendedProvider = provider.name = "mock"` (`scan.ts:118`). That value then **suppresses the entire honesty machinery**: `llmFailed = intendedProvider !== "mock"` stays `false` (`scan.ts:279`), so the "Model unavailable — showing deterministic scores" SSE event (`scan.ts:282-287`) and the `report.warnings` line "AI analysis was unavailable…" (`scan.ts:322-326`) are **both skipped**. The progress copy even reads "Scoring against the rubric…" (`scan.ts:192-194`) — no model is ever named, because none is intended.

So what Mei gets on a keyless public deploy: a report whose only mock disclosure is the corner chip "Demo · deterministic rubric" (`ReportHeader.tsx:40-44`) and a badge with a "· demo" suffix (`badge/[owner]/[repo]/route.ts:324-325`). The prominent amber "Heads up" caveat banner (`ReportView.tsx:171-183`) stays **empty** for this case — it only ever fills from `llmFailed`, token-skip, truncation, or stack-fit, none of which a keyless-mock public scan trips. Meanwhile the landing FAQ tells her "an LLM adds nuance — guardbanded to that evidence so scores stay honest" (`src/app/page.tsx:30`). For a keyless public scan, no LLM ran at all.

What the mock floor actually produces (the thing her badge would vouch for):
- **Roadmap** = a fixed 9-entry template catalog ranked by `weight × (100−score)`, top 3 (`src/lib/scoring/recommendations.ts:20-160`). Every D2-low repo gets the byte-identical "Few tests vouch for behavior" rationale. There is no per-repo reasoning.
- **Discrepancy audit** = `discrepancies: []`, always (`mock.ts:86`). The "Flagged for review / The AI auditor believes…" panel (`ReportView.tsx:245-261`) **never renders** on a mock scan — the one genuine reasoning sub-task is simply absent, silently.
- **Summaries/strengths/gaps** = lowercased signal labels joined with "; " (`mock.ts:31-39`) — readable, but visibly mechanical to a power-user.

## Surface-model notes (fresh file:line for my angle)

- Provider selection: `src/lib/llm/index.ts:61-73` (`resolveProviderChoice` default `auto`; `geminiOrMock` key-or-mock), `:140-143` (`auto`/`gemini` → `geminiOrMock`).
- Mock floor: `src/lib/llm/mock.ts:42-97`; roadmap catalog `src/lib/scoring/recommendations.ts:20-160`; empty discrepancies `mock.ts:86`.
- Disclosure-suppression on keyless mock: `scan.ts:118` (`intendedProvider`), `:279` (`llmFailed` gated on `!== "mock"`), `:322-326` (warning gated on `llmFailed`), `:192-194` (rubric copy).
- Mock disclosure that *does* exist: header chip `ReportHeader.tsx:40-44`; badge "· demo" `badge route:319-325`; empty-discrepancies panel hidden `ReportView.tsx:245`.
- Badge path's own mock: an uncached badge runs `scanRepository(..., { mock: true })` (`badge route:301`) → correctly labelled "· demo". Honest, and it prefers a real LLM cache entry when one exists (`badge route:286`).
- The AI claim Mei judges against: `src/app/page.tsx:22,30`.
- Lens-C price basis: `src/lib/llm/config.ts:39-55`; public default model `gemini-3-flash-preview` ($0.5/$3) `src/lib/llm/gemini.ts:15`.

## Lens-C answer

**cheaper-holds — but the real axis is real-vs-mock, not cheap-vs-premium.** Because scoring is guardbanded (engine ±25, 60/40 blend), the **score** is near model-insensitive: the mock's guardbanded number is probably within a band of what any model would land on, so the *number* on the badge isn't where the model earns its keep. Where it earns it is **(a) the roadmap** (mock = static catalog, `recommendations.ts`) and **(b) the discrepancy audit** (mock = always `[]`, `mock.ts:86`) — and there, the cheapest *real* model, **`gemini-3-flash` ($0.5/$3)**, already clears the floor by simply *existing as a model*: it can reference real files/commits and emit a non-empty audit, which the mock structurally cannot. So for the public tier: **don't reach for a premium model — reach for any real one.** Predicted `cost_delta` ≈ +$0.005–0.02 on the first scan of a repo (input-heavy prompt, cached thereafter), vs $0 for mock. Premium (sonnet/opus) would help only the discrepancy audit on genuinely complex repos and isn't worth ~6× the input price for a free OSS badge. The Lens-C verdict for MY tier: the mock floor is the wrong default; the right one is the cheap real model that's already configured.

## Character feedback (in Mei's voice)

**Would I trust this number?** The number, maybe — it's guardbanded to deterministic detectors, that's honest. But "trust the number" isn't the question. The question is whether the *thing under the number* — the roadmap, the audit — actually looked at my repo. On a keyless deploy, it didn't. A score I can't trace to reasoning about MY code is a vibe with a logo.

**Would I paste the badge?** Not as-is. If I scan from a deploy with no key, my "AI-native maturity" badge was generated by an `if/else` ranking table, the AI auditor never ran, and the report told me so only with a small grey chip in the corner while the front page promised "an LLM adds nuance." I'd be vouching for slop in front of thousands of strangers. Hard no until the public tier provably runs a model — or shouts "deterministic, no AI" as loudly as it shouts everything else.

**Is the roadmap one I'd run?** The mock one, no — "Few tests vouch for behavior" is the same sentence every under-tested repo gets. That's the generic next-step my senior bar explicitly rejects. A real `gemini-flash` roadmap that names my actual missing CI gate or my actual thin `AGENTS.md`? That I'd read.

**Worth the wait/cost?** A few cents and a few seconds for a credible badge beats an evening of self-assessment — easily. The cost was never the problem. *Mock to save the few cents* is the problem.

**The ONE engine change I want:** Make `auto` either run the real cheap model on the public tier, or — if there's genuinely no key — disclose the deterministic floor as loudly as the failure-degrade already does: fill the amber caveat banner, mark the badge unambiguously "deterministic (no AI)," and don't let the front page promise an LLM that didn't run. Right now the only difference between "the model failed" and "no model was ever asked" is whether you set `intendedProvider`, and only the first one is honest with the user.

**Would I tell a peer?** If the public scan ran a real model and the badge linked to traceable per-dimension evidence — yes, gladly, it'd sit next to my Scorecard badge. As shipped on a keyless deploy — I'd warn them: check whether your badge actually ran AI before you paste it.

---

- **Grounding score: 4/5.** The five major sources reach the prompt and the public path is genuinely free/no-signup. Docked one because on MY actual tier (keyless public) the *richest* grounding — the LLM's reasoning over file excerpts and commits, the whole point — never executes; the mock reads only `signalScore` + signal labels (`mock.ts:31-39, 59-78`), so most of the assembled grounding is collected and discarded.
- **Per-use time-saved: ~3 hours** (an evening of staring at my own repo with fresh eyes, collapsed to a minute) — **but only if a real model runs.** With the mock floor, time-saved drops to ~0: I can read the deterministic detector labels myself, and a generic roadmap I can't paste saves nothing.
- **Engine verdict: fix-then-ship.** The wrapping, the honest billing, the free no-signup path, and the *labelled* degrade are strong and shippable. The one blocker for my tier: the keyless-`auto` default silently serves the mock floor as an "AI" product. Make the public default a real cheap model (gemini-3-flash, already wired) or disclose the floor as loudly as a failure — then ship.
